course_description:
Introduction to field theory in physics. Topics include an overview of classical field theories, the mathematical description of field theories, and a detailed coverage of classical electromagnetism, including Maxwell's equations, and optics.

The course consists of an introduction to classical field theories in physics. Classical field theory is an important component of theoretical physics, and is the foundation of quantum field theories such as quantum electrodynamics.

The range of classical field theories is diverse, including optics, electrodynamics, fluid dynamics, elastodynamics, general relativity, and more. We will concentrate on electromagnetism, including wave optics, as it is the classical field theory of major importance in physics, but will also cover general aspects of field theories, and include specific examples from other classical field theories, including fluid flow and temperature diffusion.

The course provides part of a comprehensive, complete and coherent program of education in Physics intended for students aiming to become professional physicists. It is a compulsory course for completing the Physics Major and for entry into the fourth year honours degree. The Masters version of this course (PHYS7155) is a required course for the Master of Science (Physics) degree. The course is also suitable for students who are interested in the foundations of theoretical physics, or are interested in applications of classical field theory, such as optics.

Expected capabilities on entering the course

Conceptual understanding

(PHYS1002) Meaning of electric field, electric potential, magnetic field

(PHYS1002) Simple field configurations for electrostatic and constant current cases (point charge, infinite wire, solenoid)

Mathematical skills

(MATH1051) Able to integrate and differentiate common functions such as powers, log, exp,sin, etc

(MATH1051) Definite and indefinite integrals

(MATH1051) Manipulation of complex functions (exp ix) and trigonometric functions (sin A+B)

(MATH1052/PHYS1002) Line and surface integrals

(MATH1052) Gradient vector and gradient fields

(MATH1052) Ordinary differential equations

(MATH1052) Partial Derivatives

Problem solving and analysis

(PHYS1002) Use of Coulomb’s Law, Gauss’s Law, Ampere’s Law to solve problems for simple static and constant current situations

(PHYS1002) Ability to calculate potential from electric field in simple situations

Experimental

(PHYS1001/PHYS1002) Able to design and conduct simple experiments

(PHYS1001/PHYS1002) Able to estimate uncertainties and propagate uncertainties through calculations

(PHYS1002) An understanding of how to write a laboratory report

Expected capabilities on exiting the course

Conceptual understanding

Application of field models to situations involving temperature, pressure, gravitation, and fluid flow

Understand the meaning of each of Maxwell’s equations

Understand the wave equation, its solutions and how Maxwell’s equations lead to the wave equation for electromagnetic fields

Ability to discuss polarisation including types of polarisation, conversion between different states using polarisers and wave plates

Ability to discuss interference between two beams and between multiple beams

An understanding of the propagation of waves in a cavity including discussion of boundary conditions and modes

Operation of Michelson interferometer

Understand propagation of a wave through a dielectric material and reflection at surfaces

Mathematical skills

Use and meaning of Div, Grad, Curl

Translate between differential and integral forms of Maxwell’s equation

Problem solving and analysis

Find electric and magnetic fields of static charge or constant current situations using scalar and vector potentials. Calculate fields from potentials and vice versa

Use and manipulate harmonic waves including calculating and using wave number, angular frequency, phase and amplitude. Mathematically describe states of polarisation of an electromagnetic field

Calculate interference between two waves

Calculate guided wave numbers, and speeds for rectangular wave guides and cavities

Calculate reflection coefficients for electromagnetic waves incident on a surface

Apply the Navier-Stokes equations in different settings, including viscid and inviscid flow, and vortices.

Experimental

Able to design and conduct advanced measurements in areas relating to interference, diffraction, and polarisation

Able to use linear regression to analyse a set of experimental measurements

Able to compare and contrast a theoretical model with experimental measurements

Able to clearly communicate results to peers

In the School of Mathematics and Physics we are committed to creating an inclusive and empowering learning environment for all students. We value and respect the diverse range of experiences our students bring to their education, and we believe that this diversity is crucial for fostering a rich culture of knowledge sharing and meaningful exploration. We hold both students and staff accountable for actively contributing to the establishment of a respectful and supportive learning environment.

Bullying, harassment, and discrimination in any form are strictly against our principles and against UQ Policy, and will not be tolerated. We have developed a suite of resources to assist you in recognising, reporting, and addressing such behaviour. If you have any concerns about your experience in this course, we encourage you to tell a member of the course teaching team, or alternatively contact an SMP Classroom Inclusivity Champion (see Blackboard for contact details). Our Inclusivity Champions are here to listen, to understand your concerns, and to explore potential actions that can be taken to resolve them. Your well-being and a positive learning atmosphere are of utmost importance to us.

Additional timetable information
All classes will be conducted on campus, consult your personal timetable for times and locations.ﾠ Students are expected to attend these sessions in person unless they have a valid reason for being unable to attend (such as illness).ﾠ Alternative arrangements will be advised on Blackboard should the campus be closed for any reason.ﾠ Workshop sessions will be recorded and available via Blackboard. There are no classes on public holidays.
Important
: if you are ill, then do not attend any classes in person. Alternative arrangements can be organised – consult Blackboard for details.ﾠ
course_aims: The course aims to introduce students to classical field theory, the role of classical field theory in physics, and the application of classical field theory in both theoretical and experimental contexts.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the basic principles of field theory, and how fields are used in various areas of physics.
Apply mathematical methods of field theory to the quantitative solution of problems.
Understand the role of classical field theory in real-world problems.
Investigate classical fields experimentally, applying laboratory skills and error analysis to test theoretical predictions.
Engage in scientific and technical communication about classical field theory.

assessment_summary.title: Laboratory Report 1 (short)
assessment_summary.due_date: 5 pm, 2 weeks after completing the second short experiment (see lab schedule)

assessment_summary.title: Laboratory Report 2 (Long)
assessment_summary.due_date: 5 pm, 2 weeks after completing the experiment (see lab schedule)

assessment_details.title: Formative Quizzes
assessment_details.task_description: To get the most out of each workshop, you will need to prepare beforehand by completing a prescribed reading (approximately 30-60 minutes). Each quiz will generally consist of a short answer question related to the reading material. A mark of 1 is allocated for each quiz where the student successfully demonstrates that they have engaged with the reading. Grades (on a 1-7 scale) are awarded as per the following: 7: Successful completion of at least 10 quizzes 6: Successful completion of at least 9 quizzes 5: Successful completion of at least 8 quizzes 4: Successful completion of at least 6 quizzes 3: Successful completion of at least 4 quizzes 2: Successful completion of at least 2 quizzes 1: Successful completion of fewer than 2 quizzes Note that you need only 10 of the 12 reading quizzes to be successfully completed in order to get the full 5%. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The quiz must be submitted electronically via Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Reading quizzes are used by instructors to gauge your understanding of the material before the workshop, hence no extensions will be given for this assessment item.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Reading quizzes are used by instructors to gauge your understanding of the material before the workshop, hence no late submissions will be accepted.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Laboratory Report 1 (short)
assessment_details.due_date: 5 pm, 2 weeks after completing the second short experiment (see lab schedule)
assessment_details.task_description: Each student will be allocated into two short experiments. The experiments will be conducted on-campus. You will submit one short laboratory report for either of the two short experiments (your choice of experiment). Teaching staff will check your lab notebook as you are doing the experiment and you will be required to submit a copy as an appendix to your report. The report is limited to 4 pages (excluding a cover page and appendices). Students may discuss a draft report with staff before submission but reports must be your own work. Always cite material and concepts correctly in your submission. We prefer that such material is expressed in your own words rather than quoted directly from the source. Staff reserve the right to use Turnitin software to check the originality of all submitted material. Generative AI use is permitted but its usage must be described in an appendix. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Reports must be submitted electronically via the University's Blackboard system. Submissions must be in portable document format (i.e. a pdf file).
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
See ADDITIONAL ASSESSMENT INFORMATION for extension/deferral information relating to this assessment item.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: In-semester examination
assessment_details.task_description: Students will have an in-semester exam during the semester, scheduled for 6th May 2026, 5 pm (exam location to be advised on Blackboard). This will consist of two parts: the first covering module 1 and the second covering module 2. The exam will involve short answer and problem-solving questions - ensure that your answers include clear physics reasoning. Solutions consisting of only equations and numbers without explanations will be awarded a grade no higher than 4. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 90 minutes Calculator options (In person) Casio FX82 series only or UQ approved and labelled calculator Open/closed book Closed book examination - specified written materials permitted Materials One A4 sheet of handwritten notes, double sided, is permitted Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
See ADDITIONAL ASSESSMENT INFORMATION for extension/deferral information relating to this assessment item.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam .

assessment_details.title: End of semester examination
assessment_details.task_description: The end of semester examination in this course will be held during the end-of-semester examination period. It will be an in-person exam held on campus. The exam will consist of two parts - the first part will cover module 3 while the second part will consist of advanced questions for all three modules. Each part will involve short answer and problem-solving questions - ensure that your answers include clear physics reasoning. Solutions consisting of only equations and numbers without explanations will be awarded a grade no higher than 4. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Background and further reading Students are expected to engage in background and further reading during semester. The reading will add value to class discussions and allow lectures to focus on difficult areas encountered by students. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Workshops Workshops will begin with a brief coverage of selected core content, followed by discussion and/or class activities. The workshops will run on campus. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Laboratory The laboratory activities will help you to develop your skills at physical concepts, data analysis, and communicating results. An introductory laboratory session will be conducted in Weeks 1 and 2, and students must complete a linear regression exercise before commencing later laboratory experiments. Students will then be scheduled to two 1-week (short) experiments and one 2-week (long) experiment during the semester. See Blackboard for further details of scheduling. Opportunities for "catch-up" experiments will be available near the end of semester. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Problem-solving applied classes Solving problems is an essential part of physics. Weekly applied classes are an opportunity to practice solving problems in classical field theory, and will provide training in problem solving that will be relevant for the examinations. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
